coredata.dc:title: Synthesis of carbon dots using wild Citrullus lanatus var citroides for drug delivery applications

coredata.dc:description: Nanomaterials are widely explored in the various fields such as medicine and biomedical engineering, catalysis and environmental pollution detection. Research in nanotechnology has led to the discovery of fluorescent carbon dots (CDs), which are biocompatible, resistant to photobleaching and have high fluorescence intensity compared to traditional fluorescent probes. CDs may be synthesized from cheap carbon material and herein, this study reports an economical and environmentally friendly hydrothermal synthesis of CDs using wild melon (Citrullus lanatus var citroides) peels. The as-prepared CDs were spherical with an amorphous structure and possessed an average size of 1.2 nm. The CDs were also successfully used for bacterial cell labelling of E. coli and S. aureus, which indicated that the CDs were able to attach and possibly penetrate the cells. The interaction of bacterial cells with CDs resulted in fluorescence increase of the cells. Finally, AMP-Na was successfully conjugated to the as-prepared CDs and the conjugate (AMP-Na/CDs) exhibited enhanced antibacterial activity compared to bare CDs and bare AMPNa. The conjugate showed capability of bacterial inhibition for both Gram-positive (S. aureus) and Gram-negative (E. coli, P. aeruginosa) as well as AMP-Na resistant P. aeruginosa, where in all cases the conjugate showed substantial zones of inhibition. For this study, fluorescing CDs with good physicochemical properties, excellent bacteria labelling, and as antibacterial conjugate were successfully synthesized. Antibiotic-conjugated CDs could be used as more efficient, targeted antimicrobial drug delivery systems. Biological applications of CDs should also be extended to use in vivo in animal models as an imaging entity.

Furthermore, *** indicates statistical difference between zones of inhibitions for PA1 at clinical breakpoint concentration and **** indicates the significance difference between 64.0 μg/ml AMP-Na and 640.0 μg/ml CDs/AMP-Na inhibition zones for PA2.

head.ce:title: Synthesis of carbon dots using wild Citrullus lanatus var citroides for drug delivery applications

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Nanomaterials are widely explored in the various fields such as medicine and biomedical engineering, catalysis and environmental pollution detection. Research in nanotechnology has led to the discovery of fluorescent carbon dots (CDs), which are biocompatible, resistant to photobleaching and have high fluorescence intensity compared to traditional fluorescent probes. CDs may be synthesized from cheap carbon material and herein, this study reports an economical and environmentally friendly hydrothermal synthesis of CDs using wild melon (Citrullus lanatus var citroides) peels. The as-prepared CDs were spherical with an amorphous structure and possessed an average size of 1.2 nm. The CDs were also successfully used for bacterial cell labelling of E. coli and S. aureus, which indicated that the CDs were able to attach and possibly penetrate the cells. The interaction of bacterial cells with CDs resulted in fluorescence increase of the cells. Finally, AMP-Na was successfully conjugated to the as-prepared CDs and the conjugate (AMP-Na/CDs) exhibited enhanced antibacterial activity compared to bare CDs and bare AMPNa. The conjugate showed capability of bacterial inhibition for both Gram-positive (S. aureus) and Gram-negative (E. coli, P. aeruginosa) as well as AMP-Na resistant P. aeruginosa, where in all cases the conjugate showed substantial zones of inhibition. For this study, fluorescing CDs with good physicochemical properties, excellent bacteria labelling, and as antibacterial conjugate were successfully synthesized. Antibiotic-conjugated CDs could be used as more efficient, targeted antimicrobial drug delivery systems. Biological applications of CDs should also be extended to use in vivo in animal models as an imaging entity.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Over the past decade, scientists and researchers have developed immense interest towards application of nanomaterials in biomedicine fields such as in drug delivery, bioimaging and tissue scaffolds [1–3]. Several studies have been conducted where various nanomaterials such as semiconductor quantum dots [4], mesoporous silica [5], dendrimers [6], solid lipid nanoparticles [7], metallic nanoparticles [8], polymeric nanoparticles, [9] magnetic nanoparticles [10], graphene oxide [11], and micelles [12,13], have been successfully utilized as nano carriers and nano biomarkers in bioimaging and drug delivery. However, these nanomaterials have wide range of limitations such as poor target specific action, low biocompatibility, low biodegradability and poor solubility and cytotoxicity [13,14]. For instance, it has been reported that an increase in the concentration of graphene oxide up to 100 μg/ml could cause significant damage to the cellular membranes [11]. One of the disadvantages of mesoporous silica nanoparticles include the interaction between their silanol groups with the surface of the phospholipids of the red blood cell membranes leading to hemolysis, as well as inducing metabolic changes resulting in complications such as melanoma [5]. Side effects such as argyria have also been reported in people who have been exposed for a long time to metallic nanoparticles such as silver nanoparticles. Polymeric nanoparticles such as polyanhydrides have been applied in cancer therapy [9]. However, these nanoparticles are highly hydrophobic, making their rate of release and erosion constant and obvious, thus limiting their selectivity and specificity [9].

Due to these limitations, the search for new types of nanocarriers that possess unique physicochemical properties for application in bioimaging and drug delivery continues [15]. The discovery of carbon dots (CDs) in 2004 have proven to be promising candidates in biomedical applications. CDs have demonstrated their superiority as a new type of nanomaterials that present distinct optical and physicochemical properties such as tunable photoluminescence, non-blinking fluorescence, high fluorescence quantum yield, excellent chemical and photostability, high biocompatibility, remarkable water solubility, large Stokes shifts and low cytotoxicity [16–18]. Due to these inmitable properties, these nanomaterials have shown advantages for biological, energy and environmental applications such as catalysis, bioimaging and biosensing [19], drug delivery, medical diagnostics, anticancer and antibacterial agents [18,20–23], multicolour light emitting diode (LED) production [24], phototherapy [25], water purification, food safety, forensic science [26,27] and energy conversion [28]. In view of this, CDs are considered promising alternatives to metal-based quantum dots, mesoporous silica and magnetic nanoparticles in biomedical research fields.

Scientists and researchers have developed keen interest in the bottom-up synthesis methods such as hydrothermal treatments and microwave-assisted synthesis routes for the preparation of CDs because these methods have been found to be cheap, ease of synthesis process, accurate control of choice of precursor carbon molecules and most importantly it is environmentally friendly [26,27,29]. Furthermore, diverse biomass has been employed as natural carbon precursors for the bottom-up synthesis of CDs including various plant peels and rinds [30], various plant leaves, fruit juices and other extracts, plant parts such as coffee and ginger roots, as well as utilization of biowaste. Therefore, it is more desirable to prepare excellent-quality CDs via simple and eco-friendly methods that utilize low-cost and readily accessible natural carbon precursors [20–22].

The inherent properties of these eco-friendly CDs such as small particle size, biocompatibility and low toxicity make them easy to couple with other organic molecules. As a result, CDs have demonstrated their effectiveness and efficiency as nano carriers for drug delivery and bioimaging [20]. Progress has been made in the application of CDs for safe, effective and targeted drug delivery through conjugation and controlled release owing to CDs' excellent properties such as small size, cell membrane permeability, superior DNA/RNA condensation capacity, low toxicity, biocompatibility, excellent pharmacological properties and a broad spectrum of different routes of administration (oral, nasal and respiratory) [31–33]. The strong photoluminescence properties of CDs provide extraordinary advantages in visualizing drug accumulation, their activities at the affected site and even tracking of drug delivery pathway [34]. CDs are able to enhance the efficacy of drugs by forming image-guided nanohybrids by integrating imaging tools with the drugs. The multifunctional capabilities of CDs has, in the past few years, led to an extensive research in the development of CD-based diagnostics and theranostics [35–37]. For example, date palm fronds were used to prepare CDs and applied for bioimaging and drug delivery [38]. In a related study, quince fruit powder was utilized for preparation of CDs via microwave-assisted synthetic route and employed as carrier for drug delivery [39]. In a more recent study, CDs were fabricated from Korean ginseng root extracts and used as nano carrier for targeted drug delivery into breast cancer cells and colorectal cancer [40]. Other studies in which natural sources of carbon precursors have been used in the preparation of CDs and application in drug delivery include leaves of almondette (Buchanania lanzan) [41], crown flower (Calotropis gigantea) leaves [42], CDs have also been applied in treatment of bacterial infections either as pure CDs when synthesized from medicinal plants such as turmeric and aloe vera [24]. It is important to note and appreciate that there exist some limitations of current drug delivery strategies using carbon dots which includes control over their size, reproducibility and surface properties that limit their applications [35]. In addition, the low fluorescence quantum yield of CDs limits the biological applications and commercialization of CDs [31]. Despite these challenges, CDs continue draw scientists and researchers' attention with wide range of applications in phototherapy, bioimaging, drug/gene delivery and theranostics.

In other instances, CDs have been conjugated to antibiotics to enhance their antibacterial activities [43,44]. The process of enhancing antibiotic efficiency could bring a breakthrough in abating the devastating effects of antimicrobial resistance that the global health sector is grappling with. CD-antibiotic conjugates could improve the effectiveness of the already rendered ineffective antibiotics against antibiotic resistant strains, such as ampicillin [45]. Ampicillin (a semi-synthetic penicillin derivatives) was developed to overcome the issue of drug resistance. However, due to its extensive use in treatment of bacterial infections, it itself became ineffective to treat some bacterial as they have also developed resistance against it [46]. Therefore, the search continues for more suitable natural carbon sources for the preparation of CDs that can be conjugated to antibiotics to enhance antibacterial activities and subsequently treat antibiotic resistant bacterial strains. This study therefore seeks to widen the antibacterial applications of CDs by making a CD/Ampicillin conjugate.

Herein, we describe a facile ecofriendly hydrothermal synthesis of CDs from citron wild melon (Citrullus lanatus var. citroides) peels as well as their photoluminescent properties and antibiotic delivery applications. The as-prepared CDs demonstrated excellent optical properties, high biocompatibility and excellent stability. Additionally, ampicillin‑sodium (AMPNa) was successfully conjugated to the as-prepared CDs and the conjugate exhibited enhanced antimicrobial activity compared to bare CDs and bare AMPNa. Overall, using the idea of green synthetic approach for preparation of self-passivated CDs, this study was able to investigate the antibacterial capabilities of these nano carbon carriers. Finally, this approach utilized quick, reproducible and low-cost synthesis procedure and reinforcing the capability of CDs obtained from natural carbon sources as promising alternatives for targeted antibiotic enhancement systems.

Wild citron melons were collected from the wild in the Palapye area, Central District, Botswana and were transported to Botswana International University of Science and Technology (BIUST) chemistry laboratory for further processing. The melons were thoroughly washed with deionized water and the fruit peels carefully removed and washed again with deionized water. The fruit peels were then air-dried and ground to a fine powder using pestle and mortar. The powder was kept in airtight sterile zip-lock bags at 4 °C for further use in preparation of CDs.

AMP-Na stock solution was purchased from Duchefa Biochemie BV, VWR Life Sciences, Sanborn, NY, USA, while Mueller Hinton broth and Mueller Hinton agar were purchased from Merck KGaA, Darmstadt, Germany. The phosphate buffered saline (PBS) was procured from Oxoid Limited, Hamsphire, England while nutrient broth was obtained from CONDA pronadisa Laboratories, Madrid, Spain. Whatman filter paper of size 10–13 μm was purchased from Sigma Aldrich (St Louis, USA). E. coli (ATCC 33559), S. aureus (ATCC 25923) and P. aeruginosa (ATCC 10145) were obtained from the laboratory stock cultures. Ampicillin resistant P. aeruginosa was donated by a project in our laboratory.

The ultrapure water was produced in the laboratory using the Thermo Fisher Scientific Barnstead Pacific TII water purification system (Niederelbert, Germany). Hydrothermal synthesis was carried out using a laboratory 200 ml hydrothermal synthesis autoclave reactor with teflon chamber purchased from Biobase Bioyu Co., Ltd. (China). A laboratory furnace (Carbolite AAF 1100) with maximum temperatures of up to 1100 °C was purchased from Chorly, England. The CDs were purified using a dialysis membrane (flat width: 43 mm, average diameter: 27 mm, molecular weight cut-off (MWCO): 14000). The shape, size and distribution of the CDs were determined using atomic force microscopy (AFM) (Bruker AFM, dimension edge Scan Asyst Peak Force) in the tapping mode. In addition, a scanning electron microscopy (SEM) (JSM-7100F Field Emission Scanning Electron Microscope, Tokyo, Japan) was used to establish the topography, morphology and compositional information of the as-prepared CDs. The crystallinity or amorphous nature of the CDs were elucidated using a Bruker D8 Advance powder diffractometer (Bruker, AXS GmbH, Karlsruhe, Germany) with a Cu tube X-ray source, and a Lynx Eye XE energy-dispersive strip detector. The XRD current was set at 40 mA with tension of 40 kV using Cu-Kα radiation at a wavelength of 1.5418 Å and 2θ range of 5 to 90 o and steps of 0.02 o with a time of 0.3500 s/step. Spectroscopic investigations of the structure of CDs were carried out using a Horiba Labram Raman spectrometer (JOBIN-YVON Technology, France) bearing an excitation source (532 nm laser line) and a charge coupled device (CCD) detector. Ultra-violet and visible absorption spectroscopy studies of the CDs were recorded using a Thermo Scientific Evolution 201 spectrophotometer. Various functional groups on the surface of the CDs were determined using fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectrometer were (Thermo Scientific Nicolet iS10 FTIR) run at 32 scans and resolution of 0.50 cm−1. Photoluminescence studies were carried out using a spectrofluorometer (Perkin-Elmer LS 55, Massachusetts, USA).

The CDs were prepared via a green one-step hydrothermal procedure according to previous report with minor modification as demonstrated in Fig. 1 [46]. Briefly, the wild citron melons were washed thoroughly with deionized water to remove soil and dirt. After leaving melons to dry in the air, the peels were obtained using a sterile scalpel and washed thoroughly with deionized water. This process was repeated several times. The peels were then cut into small pieces and then using mortar and pestle. Crushed. 2 g of the crushed peels were added to 50 ml of ultrapure water. The resultant solution was transferred into a Teflon stainless steel hydrothermal autoclave and heated in a furnace at various temperatures ranging from 100 to 380 °C at a constant time of 3 h. The optimum synthesis time was also determined experimentally by carrying out the synthesis at a time range of 1 to 9 h. Finally, optimum synthesis conditions of temperature and time were determined experimentally resulting in production of high-quality fluorescent CDs. After allowing the hydrothermal autoclave to cool to room temperature, the as-prepared CDs were obtained as a yellow solution and then centrifuged for 10 min at 8000 rpm using a Thermo Scientific Heareus Mega 40 Centrifuge (Osterode am Harz, Germany). The solution was then filtered using a 0.22 μm filter membrane followed by dialysis (retained molecular weight: 1000 Da) against ultrapure water for 24 h. The resulting solution of CDs was obtained and stored at 4 °C for further characterization and biological application.

The fluorescence quantum yield (FQY) of as-prepared CDs was determined by a slightly modified protocol previously reported in literature [26]. Briefly, anthracene used as a reference standard was dissolved in ethanol. Anthracene was used because of its known FQY of fluorescence to be 0.27 at excitation wavelength of 350 nm. The emission of fluorescence by anthracene is within the region 360–480 nm which is the case with the as-prepared CDs. To calculate the FQY of CDs, absorbance and fluorescence intensities of CDs and anthracene using prepared standards were determined and substituted in eq. (1).

Lasty, η2 CDs and η2 anthracene are respective refractive indices the solvents used to dissolve CDs and anthracene. Absorbances of both anthracene and CDs were measured using a spectrophotometer at 350 nm and the absorbances were maintained under 0.1 to reduce self-absorption effect.

AMP-Na (Duchefa Biochemie BV) stock solution was prepared by dissolving 32 mg of AMP-Na to 1 ml of ultrapure water to obtain 32 mg/ml stock solution. The clinical breakpoint concentration of AMP-Na antibiotic was prepared according to Tamocha et al., (2019) and Rahube et al., (2014) protocols where the stock solution of AMP was diluted with ultrapure water to obtain a final concentration of 32 μg/ml [47,48]. To prepare AMP-CDs conjugate, a modified protocol by Thakur et al., (2014) was used and a similar procedure as the preparation of AMP was followed [43]. Briefly, 1 ml of CDs solution was used to dissolve AMP-Na to make 32 mg/ml conjugate from which 32 μg/l clinical breakpoint concentration was prepared. The solution of the CDs and AMP was then allowed to react for 3 h at room temperature in an orbital shaker (VWR 3500 L, USA) set at 120 rpm. Thereafter, the resultant conjugate reaction mixture was then dialysed against ultrapure water in a dialysis membrane (retained molecular weight: 1000 Da) to remove unreacted material. Clinical breakpoint solutions of AMP and the prepared conjugate were kept at 4 °C for characterization and antimicrobial activity experiments. Conjugation was tested by FTIR (Thermo Scientific Nicolet iS10 FTIR).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluation of antibacterial activities of the prepared AMP-Na/CDs conjugate
ce:section.ce:para: The stock solutions of both plain AMP-Na and AMP-Na/CDs were used to prepare different concentrations of 32.0 μg/l (1.2×) and increasing concentrations of 38.4 μg/l (1.4×), 44.8 μg/L (1.6×), 51.2 μg/l (1.8×) and 64.0 μg/l (2×) were utilized to determine the effect of increasing concentration of both AMP-Na and AMP-Na/CDs on the zone of inhibitions against E. coli and S. aureus. Mueller Hinton broth and Mueller Hinton agar were used for culturing test bacteria, which were obtained from the stock cultures at Department of Biological Sciences and Biotechnology, BIUST, Palapye, Botswana. For all antimicrobial activity tests the test bacteria were steaked on MH agar to obtain single colonies. Then the liquid cultures were prepared by inoculating 10 ml MH broth with a single colony of bacteria, which were then incubated in a 37 °C incubator shaker (VWR 3500 L, USA) set to 120 rpm. From the overnight liquid cultures at OD600 = 0.5, 100 μl of the cultures were spread plated on MH agar for well diffusion test method. Diffusion wells of 9 mm width and 3 mm depth were bored onto the agar using a sterile cork borer and 100 μl of prepared concentrations of AMP-Na and the AMP-Na/CDs conjugate were placed in each well. Disc diffusion experiments were carried out by cutting out 6 mm discs from a filter paper using an office puncher. The discs were then dipped into respective concentrations of AMP or conjugate. The discs were then placed on the surface of the media containing a lawn of test bacteria. All the treatments were carried out in triplicates and bare CDs were used as control against the test bacteria. All the plates were incubated at 37 °C for 24 h after which diameters of zones of inhibition were measured. The treatments were also tested against wild type P. aeruginosa (PA1) and AMP-Na resistant P. aeruginosa (PA2) following the same well diffusion method and the same concentrations as for E. coli and S. aureus.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Interaction of the as-prepared CDs with E. coli and S. aureus cells
ce:section.ce:para: Overnight cultures of E. coli and S. aureus were prepared in nutrient broth. From the overnight cultures, measuring OD600 = 0.5, 15 ml of cultures were centrifuged at 5000 rpm for 10 min and the pellets washed twice with equal volume of phosphate buffered saline (PBS) (pH: 7.4 ± 0.2). For each test organism, a bacterial pellet was re-suspended in 15 ml of PBS solution to serve as a blank and the others dissolved in 15 ml of CDs. The test tubes were then incubated in an orbital shaker at 37 °C and 120 rpm speed. A sample of 3 ml was drawn at 0, 0.5, 1, 2 and 3 h intervals and centrifuged at 5000 rpm for 10 min. The resultant pellets were washed twice with PBS followed by resuspending in 3 ml of PBS for use in spectrofluorometric analysis. Fluorescence measurements of CDs that absorbed E. coli and S. aureus were then obtained at an excitation wavelength of 350 nm within a range of 200 nm and 650 nm. Furthermore, an extra test tube containing only CDs was incubated along with the treated test tubes to determine the effect of temperature of incubation on the fluorescence emission of CDs within the incubation period. Fluorescence measurements of CDs were recorded at every sampling interval.

Statistical analysis on the zones of inhibitions of E. coli and S. aureus, PA1 and PA2 were conducted using one-way-ANOVA and Turkey's multiple comparison test (GraphPad Prism 9.5.1 software) for disc diffusion and well diffusion tests. Statistical analysis was presented as mean of inhibition zones±standard error of the mean.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Establishing optimum synthesis conditions for high quality CDs
ce:section.ce:para: During synthesis of CDs, some synthesis conditions such as temperature and time were optimised in order to obtain high quality CDs with potent photoluminescence properties. Fig. 2 (a) shows that the fluorescence intensity of the as-prepared CDs increased as the temperature increased from 100 °C to 300 °C after which fluorescence intensity reduced. The increase in fluorescence below 300 °C could suggest that carbonization of the peels was still proceeding at these temperatures, whereas reduction in fluorescence signified complete carbonization of the peels to CDs was reached at 300 °C [49]. In addition, decreased fluorescence observed beyond 300 °C could be attributed to high charring degree of the carbon material. Charring occurs at higher temperatures where the carbon material is converted to insoluble carbon residues with low oxygen and hydrogen content [50]. Thus, the carbon content in the peels could have been converted to carbon residues (chars) beyond 300 °C, hence resulting in reduced fluorescence of the CDs. Hence, 300 °C was selected as the optimum temperature for hydrothermal synthesis of the CDs from citron melon. Similarly, a gradual increase in hydrothermal synthesis time from 1 to 6 h when the temperature was set to optimum of 300 °C resulted in increased fluorescence intensity of the synthesized CDs. However, a further increase in synthesis time beyond 6 h resulted in decreased fluorescence intensity as shown in Fig. 2 (b). Therefore, all subsequent preparation of CDs in this study were carried out at the experimentally determined optimum temperature and time of 300 °C and time of 6 h respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structure and morphology of the as-prepared CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The size and physical appearances of the as-prepared CDs were studied using AFM and SEM. Fig. 3 (a) and (b) show the 2-D topographic AFM image of the CDs and their size distribution respectively. It is evident that the CDs possess a spherical morphology and have an approximate average size of 1.2 nm and good dispersibility. The particle size distribution of the CDs (Fig. 3 (b)) indicate that they are spread between 1.0 and 1.8 nm. The wide particle size distribution of the CDs may be due to agglomeration of CDs. Fig. 3 (b) also shows the height and elevation of the CDs granules on a flat mica slide. The size and morphology of the as-prepared CDs are consistent with those obtained in previous studies [51,52]. In addition, SEM was also employed to establish the morphology, topography and granular orientation information of the as-prepared CDs. The spherical structure and nanosized appearance of the prepared CDs were corroborated by the SEM image Fig. 3 (c). The SEM image shows that the as-prepared CDs are nanospheres and also exhibit agglomeration tendencies which confirms the different size distribution and varying size as indicated by the AFM and SEM pictures. The SEM images demonstrate that the nanosized CDs size range is suitable for effective drug delivery applications [53].

The XRD was employed to investigate the crystalline nature of the as-prepared CDs. The XRD pattern in Fig. 3 (d) displays a broad peak at approximately 2θ = 23° which is generally considered to be a sign of successful formation of CDs and it specifies the amorphous morphology of the as-prepared CDs without any crystalline phases [54]. The peak also corresponds to (002) lattice spacing of a carbon material [55]. This characteristic peak has been reported as a typical peak for CDs synthesized from plants material [56]. Furthermore, the results demonstrate that the as-prepared CDs reveal graphitic character with highly disordered carbon atoms [55].

The as-prepared CDs were also characterized using FTIR and Raman spectroscopy. The FTIR provided information about the functional groups found on the surface of the as-prepared CDs. The hydrophilic character and biocompatibility of the as-prepared CDs was revealed by the presence of OH stretching vibrations at 3209 cm-1 (Fig. 4 (a) [57]. The two absorption bands situated at 2920 and 2851 cm-1 are attributed to CH stretching vibrations [57]. The peak at 1691 cm-1 suggests the presence of CO carboxylic group and 1062 cm-1 corresponds to the CO group [46]. The presence of the CO group suggests the partial oxidation of CDs surfaces. The asymmetric stretching vibration of C-O-C is indicated by the band at 1263 cm-1. Finally, the CC (sp2), which confirms the existence of CDs core structure is revealed by the peak at 1577 cm-1. The bending vibration of the OH can be ascribed to the peak at 1415.1 cm-1. The absorption bands at 605 and 792 cm-1 indicate the out of plane stretching vibrations of the CH [27]. In a study conducted by Huo et al. (2020), CDs synthesized hydrothermally from grapefruit exhibited peaks at 2976 and 2900 cm-1 both attributed to CH stretching vibrations while the absorption bands at 1733 and 1572 cm-1 corresponded to CH, CO and CC functional groups respectively [58]. Raman spectroscopy was also used to study the presence of hybridized carbon atoms, which make up the inner core structure of CDs. Fig. 4 (b) shows the D-Band at 1337 cm-1, which is ascribed to the distorted CDs configuration indicating the presence of sp3 hybridized carbon. The G-Band at 1590 cm-1 is attributed to the presence of sp2 hybridized carbon atom vibrations and depicts the graphitic nature of the as-prepared CDs (Fig. 4 (b)). The prominent intensity of the G-Band indicates the presence of more sp2 than sp3 carbon atoms in the as-prepared CDs [49]. In addition, the intensity ratio (ID/IG) of 0.27 is very low which indicates the prevalence of sp2 aggregates and fewer structural defects and confirms the existence of amorphous character of the as-prepared CDs with slight graphitic nature [54,60]. Collectively, characterization of the hydrothermally carbonized wild citron melon peels using AFM, SEM, XRD, Raman and FTIR confirms successful formation of CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and characterization of the CDs/AMP-Na conjugate by FTIR and XRD
ce:section.ce:para: The formation of the CDs/AMP-Na conjugate was confirmed by comparing the bare CDs, conjugate and AMP-Na FTIR and Raman spectra. Fig. 5 depicts the changes on the original FTIR spectra of CDs and AMP-Na when the two were reacted together for 3 h. The change in spectra implies that AMP-Na attached to the CDs at various points such as those labelled in dotted lines as a, b, c, d and e during the formation of the conjugate. A band at 1670 cm−1 (line a) on AMP-Na shifted to 1649 cm−1 on the conjugate spectra. Other bands at 1311 cm−1, 1068 cm−1, 759 cm−1 and 665 cm−1 at lines b, c, d and e respectively on AMP-Na disappeared during the formation of a conjugate. A shift in the wavenumber could indicate possible conjugation of CDs to AMPNa, also concluded by Yuan et al., (2017), where they confirmed successful CDs-Doxorubicin conjugation by shifts in wavenumbers and extra peaks observed in the FTIR spectrum [27]. The other changes that could be attributed to successful attachment was observed when the 1311 cm−1 (CH) (line b) peak on the AMP-Na disappeared on the conjugate spectrum. Similarly, a peak at 1068 cm−1 (CH) (line c) on plain CDs and 1062 cm−1 (CO), disappeared on AMP-Na spectrum. Additionally, peaks at 759 cm−1 and 665 cm−1 (CS) on AMP-Na were not observed on the conjugate. Deformations and stretching associated with CH, CO and CS could indicate the possible conjugation between CDs and AMPNa. Deformations and change in vibrations of CH, CS, CO and CN were also observed by Jijie et al., (2018) in which they concluded a successful conjugation of AMP to amine functionalised CDs [61].

The CDs/AMP-Na conjugate was further characterized using XRD and compared to both AMP-Na and bare CDs. The formation of a conjugate between CDs and AMP-Na was confirmed by a shift in the CDs characteristic peak from its original position (2θ = 23°) to the left (2θ = 19°) as depicted in Fig. 6 . The other shift was observed in the AMP-Na main peak at 2θ = 17° to the right, which also reduced in intensity when a conjugate was formed. The presence of this peak also indicates that the conjugate is of amorphous nature. XRD was also used to characterize the conjugation between CDs and amoxicillin (CD-AMX) by John et al., (2020) [43]. The team concluded that the conjugate was of amorphous nature and which corroborated X-ray diffraction pattern of the CDs [27]. In addition, the shifts of peaks have been used to confirm conjugate formation between biosurfactants to CDs by Bansal et al., (2019). They observed a change of CDs peaks at 2θ = 31.7° and 2θ = 45.8° to biosurfactant conjugate peaks at 2θ = 31.92° and 2θ = 41.1° respectively, and therefore, conclusion that the shift in the peaks could be used to confirm successful modification of CDs with biosurfactants [62]. Hence, in this study, a shift in position of the broad peaks of CDs (2θ = 23°) and AMP-Na (2θ = 17°) to 2θ = 19° of the CDs/AMP-Na conjugate is attributed to possible conjugate formation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical and absorption properties of the as-prepared CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The absorption and photoluminescence characteristics of the as-prepared hydrothermally carbonized CDs were also investigated. The fluorescence spectrum showed intense fluorescence intensity of about 900 a.u demonstrating the successful formation of CDs while the uncarbonized precursor material did not produce any significant fluorescence (Fig. 7 (a)). The insert in Fig. 7 (a) reveals the dispersion of the CDs in deionized water under daylight and after irradiation by UV light at 365 nm. The as-prepared CDs solution appeared pale yellow under daylight and cyan under UV light at 365 nm. This phenomenon is attributed to the different functional groups that exist on the surface of the as-prepared CDs [63]. The as-prepared CDs revealed maximum fluorescence emission at 430 nm after excitation at 350 nm (Fig. 7 (b)). This represented a Stokes shift of 80 nm, which can have a significant impact on the practical application of the as-prepared CDs. It has been reported that a substantial Stokes shift can enhance biological imaging by minimizing background noise and amplifying the signal-to-noise ratio [63]. The UV–vis absorption spectrum of the as-prepared CDs exhibited a broad shoulder at 314–324 nm which corresponds to the n-π* transition of the carbonyl group (C=O) (Fig. 7 (c)) [64]. This transition signals the presence of heteroatom containing functional groups on the surface of the as-prepared CDs [60]. The lack of distinct absorption peaks in the UV–vis spectrum corroborates the XRD and AFM results which indicated that the as-prepared CDs possess broad particle size distribution [30].

The effect of varying excitation wavelengths (320–400 nm) on the fluorescence intensity and emission wavelength maxima of the CDs was also investigated (Fig. 7 (d)). It is observed that the emission spectra peaks are shifted to longer wavelengths in the range of 403 to 430 nm for excitation wavelengths of 320 nm to 350 nm with concomitant increase in the fluorescence intensity. As the excitation wavelength increased from 360 to 400 nm, the emission spectra peaks still occurred at longer wavelengths, however, a decrease in the fluorescence intensity was observed. Therefore, the as-prepared CDs exhibit photoluminescence properties that are excitation wavelength-dependent. The different emission spectra that occur due to varying excitation wavelengths result from the broad particle size distribution, emissive traps and functional groups on the surface of the as-prepared CDs [64]. This fascinating attribute of the as-prepared CDs to demonstrate a characteristic redshift in their emission, implies the CDs possess smaller electronic band-gaps than the starting material (Fig. 7 (d)) [65]. Other theories such as the presence of sp2 hybridized carbon core and possibilities of other existing fluorescing molecules in the synthesized CDs solution have been proposed to contribute to the photoluminescence properties of C-Dots [59,64]. The FQY of the as-prepared CDs in ultrapure water (refractive index =1.33) and anthracene dissolved in ethanol (refractive index = 0.27) was therefore calculated to be 3.1 % using a comparative method (Fig. 8 ). Eventhough the wild melon used in this study and watermelons are of the same family of Citrullus, higher values of FQY of CDs synthesized from watermelon juice and peels have been reported in literature [29]. It is suspected that there is variation in the components such as polyphenols, citrulline and alpha amino acids which contributes significantly to CDs formation [29,56]. However, it is important to note that the exact mechanism of the photoluminescence is not fully understood as a result of the multiple and complicated structure of the CDs [66].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence evaluation of as synthesized CDs and their interaction with bacterial cells
ce:section.ce:para: Owing to their biocompatible nature and high fluorescence behaviour, CDs generated were explored for biolabeling by studying their interaction with both gram-negative (E. coli) and gram-positive (S. aureus) bacterial cells. It is well known that the in their natural state, both E. coli and S. aureus cells emit autofluorescence (Fig. 9 (a) and (b) and Fig. 10 (a) and (b)), however this autofluorescence can be enhanced by treatment with CDs [67]. The fluorescence of plain CDs was significantly quenched when incubated with the respective cells (OD600nm = 0.5) with CDs (4000.0 μg/L each), however fluorescence was enhanced for the bacterial cells (Fig. 9 (a)) when excited at 350 nm. The bacterial cells' fluorescence steadily increased from 0.5 h to 1 h when treated with CDs (Fig. 9(b) and 10(b)) and then reduced beyond 1 h (37 °C). This implies that optimum interaction of these bacterial cells with CDs was reacted at 1 h at 37 °C. The decrease in the fluorescence intensity of the CD-treated cells after 1 h could be attributed to the different interactions and affinities between CDs with cellular bacterial enzymes and macroscopic structures within the cells. It could also be due to CDs diffusing out of the cells or dissociating from the cells' surface [68]. In addition, the nano size and functional groups on the CDs surface make the CDs to slowly penetrate the cells and induce more fluorescence of the bacterial cells. Moreover, since CDs possess an electron dense core, this core facilitates bioconjugation (binding of CDs to bacterial cell structures or cellular molecules), which could also result in more fluorescence emission by both bacterial cells post treatment with CDs [69]. It has been reported that when CDs penetrate bacterial cells, they disperse either in the cellular membrane, cell walls or in the cytoplasmic regions of the cells ultimately giving the cell some enhanced fluorescence [70].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Antibacterial tests of the CDs/AMP-Na conjugate
ce:section.ce:para: The as-prepared water-soluble CDs enhanced the effectiveness of the AMP-Na nanomaterial against E. coli and S. aureus due to their ability to penetrate and circulate within the bacterial cells. AMP-Na and CDs/AMP-Na conjugate were investigated for antimicrobial activities still using E. coli and S. aureus as test bacteria using disc diffusion method where bare CDs were used as a negative control. Fig. 11 (a) and (b) demonstrate visual antimicrobial activities, where bare CDs (discs (i)) had no antibacterial activity against both test bacteria, suggesting that CDs do not anyhow interfere negatively with cells' proliferation. AMP-Na was used as a positive control and this broad-spectrum antibiotic showed relatively low zones of inhibition at clinical breakpoint (32.0 μg/ml) (for both test bacteria in discs (ii) compared to the CDs/AMP-Na conjugate as shown in discs (iii)). Das et al., (2018) conducted a CDs biolabeling study on E. coli where they concluded that the prepared CDs conjugated and interacted with E. coli cell wall but showed no growth inhibition [71]. In this disc diffusion test method, the zones of inhibitions for AMP-Na and CDs/AMP-Na were measured to be 13.7 ± 0.3 mm and 20.0 ± 0.6 for E. coli respectively, and for S. aureus zones of inhibition for AMP-Na and CDs/AMP-Na were measured to be 13.0 ± 0.6 mm and 20.0 ± 0.3 mm respectively. Statistically, the inhibition zones between AMP-Na and CDs/AMP-Na were significantly different (P < 0.05), as the conjugate exhibited a strong antibacterial activity against both test bacteria, implying that there is enhanced activity of AMP-Na by conjugating it with CDs.

The antimicrobial activity of the as-prepared CDs and the CDs/AMP-Na conjugate against E. coli and S. aureus was also evaluated using the well diffusion method (Fig. 12 (a) and (b)). Similarly, the as-prepared CDs showed no antibacterial activity. However, as the concentration of AMP-Na and CDs/AMP-Na conjugate was increased beyond the clinical breakpoint with concomitant swelling of zones of inhibition for both bacterial strains. In all cases of subsequent increase in concentration, the conjugate proved to have significantly higher zones of inhibition compared to AMPNa, and the zones of inhibition at any given concentration pair were significantly different (P < 0.0001). Furthermore, it was observed in Fig. 12 (a) that eo and fo show no statistical differences thereby indicating that the strength of the conjugate at lower concentration is equivalent to the strength of bare AMP-Na even when it is at a high concentration. However, at higher concentrations (kp and mp) the conjugate exhibits no increase in the zones of inhibition even when the concentration was increased. This was also observed for S. aureus (Fig. 12(b)) where eo versus ho and gp versus jp showed no statistical differences. In a similar study, Jijie et al. (2018) conjugated ampicillin to CDs derived from citric acid, which revealed that the CDs were non-inhibitory to E. coli K12-MG 1655 reacted for 6 h. Conversely, a conjugate between AMP-Na and CDs exhibited pronounced antimicrobial activity and membrane destruction compared to bare AMPNa. The high antimicrobial activity of the CDs/AMP-Na conjugate was further attributed to its affinity to cellular membranes due to the presence of some functional groups on the CDs surface [61].

The interaction of CDs/AMP-Na conjugate with ampicillin resistant P. aeruginosa was also investigated. It was found out that bare CDs did not have any effect on the growth of both the wild type (ampicillin sensitive) (PA1) and ampicillin-resistant (PA2) P. aeruginosa strains (Fig. 13 ). The comparison between PA1 to PA2, shows that at clinical breakpoint (32 μg/ml) of AMP-Na the PA1 strain was inhibited but not the PA2 strain. The inhibition zones for AMP-Na and the conjugate at this concentration were statistically different (P < 0.0001). On the other hand, the inhibition of PA2 was only observed at 640 μg/ml for bare AMP-Na but for the conjugate the inhibition was observed at even low concentration of 160 μg/ml. This implies that the as-prepared CDs enhanced the effect of bare AMP-Na against a resistant bacterial strain. As shown in Fig. 13, for fx;ox, hy;qy and dz.;mz the zones of inhibition are the same but AMP-Na is required at a high concentration to act against PA2 compared to a lower concentration of the conjugate which elicits the same effect on PA1. Even though the AMP-Na induces the same effect on PA1 at lower concentration (gα) the conjugate shows the same effect towards PA2 but at a higher concentration (vα). It should be noticed that it is a milestone that AMP-Na at 320 μg/ml does not have inhibition effect on the PA2 while the conjugate shows a substantial elimination of a resistant PA2 at the same concentration. The results obtained signify the potential to create a CDs/AMP-Na conjugate to mitigate antibiotic resistance at reduced doses of ampicillin.

A study conducted by Ahmadian-Fard-Fini et al., (2018) confirmed that P. aeruginosa can be labelled using CDs and that a NiFe2O4‑carbon dot composite showed high degradation and antibacterial activity against P. aeruginosa, thus corroborating the results of this study [71]. P. aeruginosa has also been found to be highly sensitive to Ag/CDs conjugate, once more indicating the ability of CDs to enhance antibacterial activity of antibiotics (Habiba et al., 2015) [72]. The potential to create a conjugate of CDs/AMP-Na with enhanced antibacterial activities to mitigate antibiotic resistance has been realised in this study. However, the method to detail the mechanism of CDs/AMP-Na conjugate still needs more investigations.

In summary, we have followed a green hydrothermal approach to synthesize CDs from C. lanatus var citroides melon peels that exhibit hydroxyl and carbonyl functional groups on their surfaces. The readily available C. lanatus var citroides melon have shown great potential to generate CDs through a short and simple approach. The as-prepared CDs were water soluble and demonstrated strong photoluminescence properties with QY of 3.1 %. In addition, they exhibited wavelength-dependent fluorescence with the emission peak shifting towards longer wavelengths. They were structurally amorphous, possessing hybridized carbon core with an average size of 1.2 nm. The synthesized CDs have demonstrated to be excellent candidates for labelling E. coli and S. aureus for exposure time of 1 h. Furthermore, the CDs were conjugated with AMP-Na for drug delivery and enhanced antibiotic activity against E. coli, S. aureus, wild type P. aeruginosa and ampicillin resistant P. aeruginosa. Use of bare CDs for application in both well- and disc diffusion exhibited no antimicrobial activity against all test bacteria, an indication that the as-prepared CDs were biocompatible and non-inhibitory to cell growth. The conjugated CDs/AMP-Na when compared to bare AMP-Na exhibited high zones of inhibition for all test bacteria, thus indicating enhanced antimicrobial activity. Successful biolabeling of bacterial cells with CDs and CDs conjugation with AMP-Na provides a gateway for future imaging and drug delivery studies.

Ketumile Ishmael Magowe: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Pogisego Dinake: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Resources. Lesedi Lebogang: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Supervision, Conceptualization.
